feynman.0: Two machines run on this whole area of study, and underneath they're the same idea. There's the DC motor, which takes the force on a current-carrying coil and turns it into something that keeps spinning round and round. And there's the particle accelerator, which uses electric fields to speed charged particles up and magnetic fields to steer them where you want them to go.
feynman.1: A DC motor takes electrical energy and turns it into spinning. The simplest one you can imagine is just a rectangular coil — n turns of wire — sitting on an axle between the poles of a permanent magnet, hooked up to a DC supply. Figure 5.1 shows where we always start it: the plane of the coil lying flat along the uniform field, parallel to it.
feynman.2: Now look at the two sides of the coil that run across the field — the ones labelled AB and CD in Figure 5.1. The current goes one way down one side and the opposite way down the other, so the field pushes on them with equal and opposite forces. Each force has the size
feynman.3: In the position drawn, one of those forces shoves straight up and the other straight down, and they're pulling at opposite edges, the full width of the coil apart. That's the best you can do for twisting — this is where the torque about the axle is biggest. The other two sides, BC and DA, run along the field instead of across it, so the field doesn't push on them at all. No force whatsoever.
feynman.4: Turn it a quarter of the way round and the plane of the coil is now standing perpendicular to the field. The two forces haven't changed size one bit, but now they pull straight in line with the coil — right at the axle, or straight away from it — so there's nothing left to twist with. The torque is zero. This is the dead spot. And here's the neat part: at that very instant the gaps in the commutator slide under the brushes and cut the current for a moment. Park a coil here at rest and it would just sit there forever. But a coil that's already spinning sails right through, carried by its own momentum.
feynman.5: Now suppose the current never switched direction. Once you're past the dead spot, the torque would start twisting the other way, and the coil would swing back — it would just rock back and forth about the perpendicular position instead of going round. The split-ring commutator is the thing that stops that from happening.
feynman.6: The commutator is a conducting ring sliced into two halves, bolted to the axle, with each half wired to one end of the coil. Stationary brushes press against it as it spins, feeding the current in from the supply. Every half turn, each brush jumps across the gap from one half-ring to the other, and that flips the current through the coil. Flip the current and you flip the forces — so the torque keeps twisting the same way round, turn after turn.
feynman.7: Don't confuse a split-ring commutator with a pair of slip rings — they're not the same thing. Slip rings are continuous, unbroken: each end of the coil keeps its very own ring the whole time, so the current through the coil never flips. Bolt slip rings onto a DC motor and you get exactly the disaster we just described — the coil rocking back and forth about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong to alternators, the AC generators you'll meet in Area of Study 3.
feynman.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator flips the coil current every half turn, right as the coil swings through the spot where the torque is zero, so that the torque always keeps twisting the same way round.
feynman.9: Trap A motor and a generator are two different machines — keep them straight. A motor uses the force a magnetic field puts on a current-carrying wire (F = nIlB) to make something spin; a generator does the reverse, using spinning to push a current into being. If you try to explain how a DC motor works by talking about electromagnetic induction, or flux, or EMF, you've reached for the wrong machine entirely.
feynman.10: Lucia builds a simple DC motor to show her class. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns, each side 5.0 cm long, and it carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, just as drawn in Figure 5.1. Work out how big the force on side AB is and which way it points, and say how big the force on side BC is.
feynman.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs across the field, so F = nIlB is the formula we want. Put the numbers in, with the side length switched into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. So the field pushes on side AB with 0.60 N, straight up by the right-hand slap rule — and on side CD with 0.60 N, straight down.
feynman.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs along the field, not across it, so the magnetic force on it is zero. Sides BC and DA give you no force and no torque at all; all they do is join up the two sides that are actually doing the pushing.
feynman.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 you'll run this same coil-in-a-field backwards. Grab the coil and twist it with an outside torque, and instead of a motor you've got a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor turning one way becomes the thing that keeps a DC generator's output voltage pointing one way — and slip rings show up again as the AC alternative.
feynman.14: The study design only asks you for the qualitative effect — which way it goes, not by how much — of three quantities on the torque of a simple motor. And all three work through the very same handle, F = nIlB: anything that makes the force on the coil sides bigger makes the torque bigger.
feynman.15: Be careful to tell the force apart from the torque as the coil turns. The force on each pushing side stays exactly the same size, nIlB, at every angle — because n, I, l and B don't care which way the coil happens to be facing. It's the torque that changes: biggest when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, zero when it stands perpendicular.
feynman.16: Trap Force isn't torque. Go right round once and the force on a pushing side holds the same steady size, nIlB, only flipping direction at each commutation; the torque, meanwhile, rises and falls as the lever arm changes. Plot the size of the force against time and you get a flat line — not a sine curve.
feynman.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is built out of just two ingredients. A uniform electric field speeds a charged particle up along a straight line, piling on kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends its path, swinging it in a new direction without touching its speed.
feynman.18: The straight-line part is just the electron gun from the electric fields topic — or the same idea scaled way up, the linear accelerator (linac). A charge q shoved from rest through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet writes in its from-rest form:
feynman.19: The bending part is the physics from the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving square across a uniform magnetic field feels a force of fixed size, F = qvB on formula sheet, always pointing dead at right angles to where it's heading — so it goes round in a circle of radius
feynman.20: And because that magnetic force points square across the velocity at every single instant, it does no work on the particle. Magnetic fields change which way you go; only electric fields change how fast.
feynman.21: Kenji runs a teaching accelerator. Electrons are sped up from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then sent into a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT pointing square across their velocity. Don't worry about relativistic effects here. Find how fast the electrons are going as they leave the gun, and the radius of the circle they trace out in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C off the formula sheet.
feynman.22: Speed. Rearrange ½mv² = qV and you get v = √(2qV/m). Drop the numbers in: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. So the electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — under a tenth of the speed of light, slow enough that we can stick with the classical treatment.
feynman.23: Radius. First switch the field strength over: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons curve round an arc of radius 0.11 m, and the magnetic force swings their direction around without ever changing their speed.
feynman.24: The Australian Synchrotron out in Clayton takes these same two ingredients and builds them up big, as Figure 5.2 shows. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields keep boosting the electrons' kinetic energy in one W = qV kick after another. A booster ring then drives them up to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they're injected into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets lay down the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam all the way round the loop.
feynman.25: The booster ring is where the one bit of synchrotron physics the study design names actually happens. As the electrons' momentum mv climbs, r = mv/qB wants the radius of their circle to climb right along with it — unless you crank B up to match. So a synchrotron ramps up the strength of its bending field in step with the particles' momentum, holding the radius fixed so the beam stays tucked inside the one ring of magnets. That keeping-in-step is exactly where the machine gets its name.
feynman.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons are doing better than 99.999% of the speed of light, and up there the classical relation r = mv/qB (only good for v ≪ c) stops working cleanly. Unit 4 digs into why the people who design accelerators have to build special relativity right into machines like this one — and why charges forced round in a circle pour out the intense synchrotron light that the whole facility exists to make.
feynman.27: This area of study circles back to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring are both just answers to the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics is identical except in one single place — which force is doing the job of the centripetal force.
feynman.28: Two rows are the ones that matter. Neither force ever changes the speed of the thing it's steering, because both of them point square across the velocity at every instant: each object travels at constant speed, but not constant velocity. The mass row is the one genuine difference — the satellite's mass cancels straight out of its orbit, while the particle's mass stays put in r = mv/qB.
feynman.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron are answers to the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity does the centripetal job for one, the magnetic force for the other, and neither force changes the object's speed.

sagan.0: Two machines grow out of this single idea, born of the same magnetic force that pushes upon moving charge. There is the DC motor, which gathers up the force on a current-carrying coil and spins it into endless rotation; and there is the particle accelerator, which calls upon electric fields to drive charged particles ever faster, and upon magnetic fields to bend and shepherd them around their course.
sagan.1: A DC motor takes the silent flow of electrical energy and turns it into motion — the energy of rotation itself. In its simplest form it is nothing more than a rectangular coil of n turns, mounted upon an axle between the poles of a permanent magnet, and joined to a DC supply. Figure 5.1 shows the coil in its familiar starting place, with the plane of the coil lying parallel to the uniform field.
sagan.2: Look now at the two sides of the coil that run across the field, perpendicular to it — the sides labelled AB and CD in Figure 5.1. They carry equal currents, but in opposite directions, and so the field presses upon them with equal and opposite forces, each of magnitude
sagan.3: In the position shown, one of these forces lifts upward and the other presses downward, and their lines of action stand apart by the full width of the coil: here, with the longest possible lever, this pair of forces yields the greatest torque the axle will ever feel. The other two sides, BC and DA, carry their current along the field or against it, and so the field exerts upon them no magnetic force whatever.
sagan.4: After a quarter turn the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field. The two forces are no weaker than before, but now they pull directly in line with the coil, straight toward or away from the axle, and so the torque falls to zero. This is the dead spot — and at that very instant the gaps in the commutator slip beneath the brushes, cutting the current for a moment. A coil left at rest here would never stir to life; only a coil already turning is carried through, borne onward by its own momentum.
sagan.5: Were the current never to change its direction, then beyond the dead spot the torque would turn against the motion, and the coil would swing back upon itself, rocking forever about the perpendicular position rather than rotating freely. The split-ring commutator is what saves it from this fate.
sagan.6: The commutator is a conducting ring, split into two halves and fixed to the axle, with each half joined to one end of the coil; stationary brushes press against the turning ring to carry current in from the supply. Once every half revolution each brush crosses the gap and passes from one half-ring to the other, reversing the current through the coil. And reversed current means reversed forces, so that the torque goes on acting always in the same rotational direction.
sagan.7: A split-ring commutator is not the same creature as a pair of slip rings. Slip rings are unbroken: each end of the coil keeps its own ring for all time, so the current through the coil never reverses. Fit them to a DC motor and they deliver precisely the failure we have just imagined — that endless rocking about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong instead to alternators, the AC generators we shall meet in Area of Study 3.
sagan.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current once every half revolution, just as the coil passes through the place where the torque vanishes, so that the torque forever acts in the one same rotational direction.
sagan.9: Trap A motor and a generator are not the same machine, and we must never confuse them. A motor takes the force a magnetic field exerts upon a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) and from it makes rotation; a generator takes rotation and from it induces a current. To explain how a DC motor works by speaking of electromagnetic induction, of flux, or of EMF is to mistake one machine entirely for the other.
sagan.10: Lucia builds a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as shown in Figure 5.1. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on side AB by the magnetic field, and state the magnitude of the force exerted on side BC.
sagan.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs perpendicular to the field, and so F = nIlB is ours to use. Substituting, with the side length first turned into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. The field exerts upon side AB a force of 0.60 N, directed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; upon side CD it exerts 0.60 N vertically downward.
sagan.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs parallel to the field, and so the magnetic force upon it is nothing at all. Sides BC and DA give neither force nor torque; they serve only to close the circuit between the two sides that carry the force.
sagan.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 this very same coil-in-field geometry runs the other way around: turn the coil by some outside torque, and it becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor turning in one direction becomes the part that keeps a DC generator's output voltage pointing one way, and slip rings return to us as the AC alternative.
sagan.14: The study design asks only that we know, in a qualitative way, how three quantities bear upon the torque of a simple motor. All three reach the torque through one door, F = nIlB: whatever increases the force upon the coil's sides increases the torque along with it.
sagan.15: Take care to hold apart the force and the torque as the coil turns. The force upon each force-carrying side keeps the very same magnitude nIlB at every angle, for n, I, l and B care nothing for how the coil is oriented. It is the torque that changes: greatest when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, vanishing when it stands perpendicular.
sagan.16: Trap Force is not torque. Through one whole revolution the force upon a force-carrying side holds the constant magnitude nIlB, reversing its direction only at each commutation; yet the torque rises and falls as the lever arm lengthens and shrinks. Plotted against time, the force magnitude is a flat line — never a sine curve.
sagan.17: Every particle accelerator in this course, however grand, is built from just two ingredients. A uniform electric field drives a charged particle forward in a straight line, swelling its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path, turning its direction aside while leaving its speed untouched.
sagan.18: The straight-line stage is the electron gun we met among the electric fields, or its grander cousin, the linear accelerator — the linac. A charge q driven from rest through a potential difference V gains the kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet sets down in its from-rest form:
sagan.19: The bending stage is the very physics of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field feels a force of unchanging magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, always at right angles to its velocity, and so it sweeps around in a circle of radius
sagan.20: Because the magnetic force stands perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, it does no work upon the particle at all. Magnetic fields turn the direction; only electric fields change the speed.
sagan.21: Kenji operates a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT directed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Determine the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
sagan.22: Speed. Rearranging ½mv² = qV gives us v = √(2qV/m). Substituting: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — under one-tenth of the speed of light, slow enough that the classical treatment serves us faithfully.
sagan.23: Radius. First we turn the field strength into tesla: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons trace a circular arc of radius 0.11 m, and the magnetic force bends their direction while leaving their speed unchanged.
sagan.24: The Australian Synchrotron at Clayton brings these two ingredients together on a vast scale, as shown in Figure 5.2. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields raise the electrons' kinetic energy through repeated W = qV stages. A booster ring then carries them up to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they are sent into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam around its circuit.
sagan.25: The booster ring holds the one piece of synchrotron physics the study design names for us. As the electrons' momentum mv swells, r = mv/qB demands that the radius of their circular path swell along with it — unless B rises in step. And so a synchrotron strengthens its bending field in synchrony with the particles' momentum, holding the radius fixed so that the beam keeps within the single ring of magnets. It is from this very synchronisation that the machine takes its name.
sagan.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons race at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, where the classical relation r = mv/qB (true only for v ≪ c) no longer holds cleanly. In Unit 4 we shall see why those who design accelerators must build special relativity into machines such as this — and why charges driven around a circle pour out the intense synchrotron light the whole facility exists to make.
sagan.27: This area of study brings us back to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring are both, at heart, solutions to the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics differs in just one place — in which force it is that supplies the centripetal pull.
sagan.28: Two rows carry the meaning. Neither force ever alters the speed of the thing it steers, for both act perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object moves at constant speed, yet never at constant velocity. The mass row is the one true asymmetry between them — the satellite's mass cancels clean out of its orbit, while the particle's mass lingers on within r = mv/qB.
sagan.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron solve one and the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity supplies the centripetal force for the one, the magnetic force for the other, and neither force ever changes the object's speed.

tyson.0: Two machines run on this area of study, and they’re both just the magnetic force wearing a costume. First, the DC motor — it takes the force on a current-carrying coil and spins it into endless rotation. Second, the particle accelerator — electric fields to crank charged particles faster, magnetic fields to steer them. Same physics, two very different machines.

tyson.3: Right now, one force shoves vertically up, the other vertically down, and their lines of action are pried apart by the full width of the coil. That’s the recipe for maximum torque about the axle. Meanwhile the other two sides, BC and DA, run their current parallel or antiparallel to the field — they feel no magnetic force at all. Nothing.
tyson.4: Give it a quarter turn and the plane of the coil now stands perpendicular to the field. The forces haven’t shrunk one bit — same magnitude — but they now pull straight along the coil, dead toward or away from the axle. Result: zero torque. This is the dead spot. And at that exact instant the gaps in the commutator slide under the brushes and snip the current for a moment. tyson.5: Here’s the catch. If the current never flipped, the torque past the dead spot would swing the other rotational way, and the coil would lurch back — rocking around the perpendicular position instead of going around. The split-ring commutator shuts that down.
tyson.6: The commutator is a conducting ring sliced into two halves, bolted to the axle, with each half wired to one end of the coil. Stationary brushes press onto the spinning ring and feed current in from the supply. Every half revolution each brush jumps the gap and switches from one half-ring to the other — flipping the current through the coil. Flip the current, flip the forces, and the torque keeps pushing in the same rotational direction. That’s the whole trick.
tyson.7: Don’t confuse a split-ring commutator with a pair of slip rings — they are not the same animal. Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil keeps its own ring for good, so the current through the coil never reverses. Bolt slip rings onto a DC motor and you get exactly the disaster described above — oscillation about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong somewhere else: alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.

curie.0: Two machines apply this area of study, and both rest on a single force. The DC motor turns the force on a current-carrying coil into continuous rotation; the particle accelerator uses electric fields to raise the speed of charged particles and magnetic fields to steer them. I will follow each in turn.
curie.1: A DC motor transforms electrical energy into energy of rotation. The simplest version I can describe is a rectangular coil of n turns mounted on an axle between the poles of a permanent magnet and connected to a DC supply. Figure 5.1 shows the standard starting position, with the plane of the coil parallel to the uniform field.
curie.2: Consider the two sides of the coil that run perpendicular to the field, labelled AB and CD in Figure 5.1. They carry equal currents in opposite directions, so the field exerts equal and opposite forces upon them, each of magnitude
curie.3: In the position shown, one force acts vertically upward and the other vertically downward, and their lines of action are separated by the full width of the coil: this pair produces the maximum torque about the axle. The other two sides, BC and DA, carry current parallel or antiparallel to the field and experience no magnetic force at all.
curie.4: After a quarter turn the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field. The two forces are unchanged in magnitude, but they now act in line with the coil, directly toward or away from the axle, so the torque is zero. This position is the dead spot — and at the same instant the gaps in the commutator pass under the brushes, momentarily cutting the current. A coil left at rest here would never start; a spinning coil is carried through by its own momentum.
curie.5: Were the current direction never to change, the torque beyond the dead spot would act in the opposite rotational sense and the coil would swing back, oscillating about the perpendicular position instead of rotating. The split-ring commutator is what prevents this.
curie.6: The commutator is a conducting ring split into two halves, fixed to the axle, with each half connected to one end of the coil; stationary brushes press against the rotating ring to carry current in from the supply. Every half revolution each brush crosses the gap and swaps from one half-ring to the other, reversing the current through the coil. Reversed current means reversed forces, so the torque continues to act in the same rotational direction.
curie.7: A split-ring commutator is not the same component as a pair of slip rings, and the distinction matters. Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil keeps its own ring permanently, so the current through the coil never reverses. Fitted to a DC motor, slip rings produce exactly the failure I described above — oscillation about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong to alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.
curie.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution, as the coil passes through the zero-torque position, so that the torque always acts in the same rotational direction.
curie.9: Trap A motor and a generator are different machines, and I keep them apart. A motor uses the force exerted on a current-carrying conductor by a magnetic field (F = nIlB) to produce rotation; a generator uses rotation to induce a current. To explain DC motor operation in terms of electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF is a category error.
curie.10: Lucia builds a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as shown in Figure 5.1. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on side AB by the magnetic field, and state the magnitude of the force exerted on side BC.
curie.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs perpendicular to the field, so F = nIlB applies. Substituting, with the side length converted to metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. The force exerted on side AB by the field is 0.60 N, directed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; the force exerted on side CD is 0.60 N vertically downward.
curie.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs parallel to the field, so the magnetic force exerted upon it is zero. Sides BC and DA contribute no force and no torque; they merely complete the circuit between the force-carrying sides.
curie.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 the same coil-in-field geometry runs in reverse: turned by an external torque, the coil becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor turning one way becomes the component that keeps a DC generator's output voltage one way, and slip rings reappear as the AC alternative.
curie.14: The study design asks only for the qualitative effect of three quantities on the torque of a simple motor. All three act through F = nIlB: whatever increases the force on the coil sides increases the torque.
curie.15: I distinguish carefully between the force and the torque as the coil rotates. The force on each force-carrying side keeps the same magnitude nIlB at every angle, because n, I, l and B do not depend on the coil's orientation. It is the torque that varies: maximum when the plane of the coil is parallel to the field, zero when it is perpendicular.
curie.16: Trap Force is not torque. Over one revolution the force on a force-carrying side keeps the constant magnitude nIlB, reversing direction only at each commutation; the torque rises and falls as the lever arm changes. Graphed against time, the force magnitude is a flat line, not a sine curve.
curie.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is built from two ingredients, and I find it clarifying to name them plainly. A uniform electric field accelerates a charged particle in a straight line, increasing its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path, changing its direction without changing its speed.
curie.18: The straight-line stage is the electron gun of the electric fields topic, or its scaled-up version, the linear accelerator (linac). A charge q accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet writes in its from-rest form:
curie.19: The bending stage is the physics of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field experiences a force of constant magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, always at right angles to its velocity, so it travels in a circle of radius
curie.20: Because the magnetic force stays perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, it does no work on the particle. Magnetic fields change direction; only electric fields change speed. This division is exact, and I rely on it throughout.
curie.21: Kenji operates a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT directed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Determine the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
curie.22: Speed. Rearranging ½mv² = qV gives v = √(2qV/m). Substituting: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — below one-tenth of the speed of light, slow enough for the classical treatment to hold.
curie.23: Radius. I convert the field strength first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons follow a circular arc of radius 0.11 m, and the magnetic force changes their direction without changing their speed.
curie.24: The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton combines these two ingredients on a large scale, as shown in Figure 5.2. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields raise the electrons' kinetic energy in repeated W = qV stages. A booster ring then brings them to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they are injected into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam around the circuit.
curie.25: The booster ring contains the one piece of synchrotron physics the study design names, and it follows directly from what I have already measured. As the electrons' momentum mv grows, r = mv/qB requires the radius of their circular path to grow too — unless B grows in step. A synchrotron therefore increases the strength of its bending field in synchrony with the particles' momentum, holding the radius constant so that the beam stays inside the one ring of magnets. This synchronisation gives the machine its name.
curie.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons travel at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, where the classical relation r = mv/qB (valid for v ≪ c) no longer applies cleanly. Unit 4 examines why accelerator designers must build special relativity into machines like this one — and why charges forced around a circle radiate the intense synchrotron light the facility exists to produce.
curie.27: This area of study returns to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring are both solutions of the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics differs in exactly one place — which force supplies the centripetal force.
curie.28: Two rows are significant. Neither force ever changes the speed of the object it steers, because both act perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object moves at constant speed but not constant velocity. The mass row is the one real asymmetry — the satellite's mass cancels out of its orbit, while the particle's mass remains in r = mv/qB.
curie.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron solve the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity supplies the centripetal force for one, the magnetic force for the other, and neither force changes the object's speed.

cox.0: Two machines grow out of this area of study, and I find them rather lovely together. There is the DC motor, which takes the force on a current-carrying coil and coaxes it into endless turning; and there is the particle accelerator, which uses electric fields to drive charged particles faster and faster, and magnetic fields to gently steer them where they need to go.
cox.1: A DC motor takes electrical energy and turns it, quite literally, into spinning. The simplest version is wonderfully bare: a rectangular coil of n turns, mounted on an axle between the poles of a permanent magnet and wired to a DC supply. Figure 5.1 shows us the standard starting position, with the plane of the coil lying parallel to the uniform field.
cox.2: Look at the two sides of the coil that run across the field, the ones labelled AB and CD in Figure 5.1. They carry equal currents in opposite directions, so the field pushes on them with equal and opposite forces, each of magnitude
cox.3: In the position shown, one force lifts vertically upward and the other presses vertically downward, and their lines of action are held apart by the full width of the coil: this pairing gives us the greatest turning effort, the maximum torque about the axle. The other two sides, BC and DA, carry their current along the field rather than across it, and so feel no magnetic force at all.
cox.4: After a quarter turn the plane of the coil sits perpendicular to the field. The two forces are just as strong as before, but now they pull straight in line with the coil, directly toward or away from the axle, and so the torque falls to zero. This is the dead spot — and at that very instant the gaps in the commutator slide under the brushes, briefly cutting the current. A coil left at rest here would never get going; a coil already spinning is simply carried through by its own momentum.
cox.5: If the current never changed direction, the torque just past the dead spot would turn the coil the other way, and it would swing back, rocking forever about the perpendicular position instead of going round. The split-ring commutator is what saves us from this.
cox.6: The commutator is a conducting ring split into two halves, fixed to the axle, with each half joined to one end of the coil; stationary brushes press against the turning ring to carry current in from the supply. Every half revolution each brush crosses the gap and steps from one half-ring across to the other, reversing the current through the coil. Reverse the current and you reverse the forces, and so the torque keeps pushing the coil round the same way.
cox.7: A split-ring commutator is not the same creature as a pair of slip rings. Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil keeps its own ring for good, so the current through the coil never reverses. Fit slip rings to a DC motor and you get exactly the failure we just saw — that endless rocking about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong instead to alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.
cox.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution, just as the coil passes through the zero-torque position, so that the torque always keeps pushing in the same rotational direction.
cox.9: Trap A motor and a generator are quite different machines, and it is worth holding them apart. A motor uses the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) to make things spin; a generator uses spinning to bring a current into being. Explaining how a DC motor works in terms of electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF is a genuine confusion of categories.
cox.10: Lucia builds a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as shown in Figure 5.1. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on side AB by the magnetic field, and state the magnitude of the force exerted on side BC.
cox.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs across the field, so F = nIlB is the one we want. Substituting, with the side length put into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. The force the field exerts on side AB is 0.60 N, pointing vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; the force on side CD is 0.60 N vertically downward.
cox.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs along the field, so the magnetic force on it is zero. Sides BC and DA give no force and no torque; they simply complete the circuit between the two sides that do the work.
cox.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 this very same coil-in-field geometry runs backwards: turn the coil with an outside torque, and it becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor spinning one way becomes the part that keeps a DC generator's output voltage pointing one way, and the slip rings step back in as the AC alternative.
cox.14: The study design asks only for the qualitative effect of three quantities on the torque of a simple motor. All three reach in through F = nIlB: anything that strengthens the force on the coil sides strengthens the torque along with it.
cox.15: It is worth being careful here to keep the force and the torque apart as the coil turns. The force on each working side stays at the same magnitude nIlB at every angle, because n, I, l and B have nothing to do with how the coil happens to be oriented. The torque is what changes: greatest when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, vanishing when it sits perpendicular.
cox.16: Trap Force is not torque. Over one full turn the force on a working side keeps the constant magnitude nIlB, flipping direction only at each commutation; the torque, meanwhile, rises and falls as the lever arm opens and closes. Plotted against time, the force magnitude is a flat line, not a gentle sine curve.

A uniform electric field drives a charged particle along a straight line, adding to its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path, turning its direction without ever touching its speed.

A charge q accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet sets down in its from-rest form:
cox.19: The bending stage is the physics of the magnetic fields topic, returning. A particle moving across a uniform magnetic field feels a force of constant magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, always turned at right angles to its velocity, and so it sweeps round a circle of radius
cox.20: Because that magnetic force sits perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant, it does no work on the particle at all. Magnetic fields change direction; only electric fields change speed.
cox.21: Kenji operates a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT directed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Determine the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.

cox.23: Radius. Put the field strength into the right units first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. cox.24: The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton brings these two ingredients together on a grand scale, as shown in Figure 5.2. A booster ring then carries them up to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they are injected into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam around the loop.

greene.0: Two machines bring this entire area of study to life. greene.1: A DC motor is a converter: it takes electrical energy and turns it—quite literally—into the energy of rotation. Picture the simplest version of all: a rectangular coil of n turns, mounted on an axle, suspended between the poles of a permanent magnet and wired to a DC supply. Figure 5.1 shows the standard starting position, with the plane of the coil lying parallel to the uniform field.
greene.2: Now focus on the two sides of the coil that run perpendicular to the field, the pair labelled AB and CD in Figure 5.1. They carry equal currents, but flowing in opposite directions, so the field pushes on them with equal and opposite forces—each one of magnitude
greene.3: In the position drawn here, one of those forces pushes straight up while the other pushes straight down, and their lines of action are held apart by the full width of the coil. This is the arrangement that produces the maximum possible torque about the axle. The remaining two sides, BC and DA, carry their current parallel or antiparallel to the field, and so feel no magnetic force whatsoever.
greene.4: Follow the coil through a quarter turn and its plane now lies perpendicular to the field. The two forces are just as strong as before, but they now act in line with the coil, pulling directly toward or away from the axle—and so the torque vanishes to zero. This is the dead spot. At that very same instant the gaps in the commutator slide beneath the brushes and momentarily snip the current. A coil left at rest here would never begin to turn; a coil already spinning is carried straight through by its own momentum.
greene.5: Here is the difficulty. If the current never reversed, then just past the dead spot the torque would suddenly push in the opposite rotational sense, and the coil would swing back—rocking endlessly about the perpendicular position rather than spinning around it. The split-ring commutator is the elegant device that stops this from happening.
greene.6: The commutator is a conducting ring sliced cleanly into two halves, fixed to the axle, with each half wired to one end of the coil; stationary brushes press against the turning ring and ferry current in from the supply. Every half revolution, each brush crosses the gap and hops from one half-ring to the other, reversing the current through the coil. Reverse the current and you reverse the forces—so the torque keeps pushing in the very same rotational direction, turn after turn.
greene.7: It is worth being clear that a split-ring commutator is not the same thing as a pair of slip rings. Slip rings are continuous, unbroken: each end of the coil keeps its own ring permanently, so the current through the coil never reverses at all. Fit slip rings to a DC motor and you reproduce exactly the failure we just described—the coil oscillating about the perpendicular position, going nowhere. Slip rings belong instead to alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.

greene.9: Trap A motor and a generator are different machines, and it pays to keep them apart. A motor takes the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) and turns it into rotation; a generator does the reverse, taking rotation and inducing a current. To explain how a DC motor works by reaching for electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF is to confuse one machine for its mirror image—a category error.

At the same instant the gaps in the commutator pass under the brushes and cut the current. A coil left at rest here would never start. A spinning coil is carried through by its own momentum.
hawking.5: Suppose the current never changed direction. Beyond the dead spot the torque would act the other way, and the coil would swing back, oscillating about the perpendicular position instead of turning. The split-ring commutator stops this from happening.
hawking.6: The commutator is a conducting ring, split into two halves, fixed to the axle, each half wired to one end of the coil. Stationary brushes press against the turning ring and carry current in from the supply. Every half revolution each brush crosses the gap and swaps from one half-ring to the other. The current through the coil reverses. Reversed current means reversed forces, so the torque keeps acting in the same rotational direction.
hawking.7: A split-ring commutator is not a pair of slip rings. Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil keeps its own ring, permanently, so the current never reverses. Fit them to a DC motor and you get exactly the failure described above — oscillation about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong elsewhere, to alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.
hawking.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution, as the coil passes through the zero-torque position, so the torque always acts in the same rotational direction.
hawking.9: Trap A motor and a generator are different machines. A motor uses the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) to produce rotation. A generator uses rotation to induce a current. Explain a DC motor in terms of induction, flux or EMF and you have made a category error.
hawking.10: Lucia builds a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as shown in Figure 5.1. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on side AB by the magnetic field, and state the magnitude of the force exerted on side BC.
hawking.11: Force on AB. The current in AB is perpendicular to the field, so F = nIlB applies. Convert the side length to metres and substitute: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. The force on AB is 0.60 N, directed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule. The force on CD is 0.60 N, vertically downward.
hawking.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs parallel to the field, so the magnetic force on it is zero. Sides BC and DA contribute no force and no torque. They merely complete the circuit between the force-carrying sides.
hawking.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 the same coil-in-field geometry runs backwards. Turn the coil with an external torque and it becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor turning one way becomes the component that keeps a DC generator's output voltage one way, and slip rings reappear as the AC alternative.
hawking.14: The study design asks only for the qualitative effect of three quantities on the torque of a simple motor. All three act through F = nIlB. Anything that increases the force on the coil sides increases the torque.
hawking.15: Keep the force and the torque apart as the coil rotates. The force on each force-carrying side holds the same magnitude nIlB at every angle, because n, I, l and B do not depend on the coil's orientation. The torque is what changes: maximum when the plane of the coil is parallel to the field, zero when it is perpendicular.
hawking.16: Trap Force is not torque. Over one revolution the force on a force-carrying side holds the constant magnitude nIlB, reversing direction only at each commutation. The torque rises and falls as the lever arm changes. Plotted against time, the force magnitude is a flat line, not a sine curve.
hawking.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is built from two ingredients. A uniform electric field accelerates a charged particle in a straight line, raising its kinetic energy. A uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path, changing its direction without changing its speed.
hawking.18: The straight-line stage is the electron gun of the electric fields topic, or its scaled-up version, the linear accelerator (linac). A charge q accelerated from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet writes in its from-rest form:
hawking.19: The bending stage is the physics of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field feels a force of constant magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, always at right angles to its velocity. It travels in a circle of radius
hawking.20: Because the magnetic force is perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, it does no work on the particle. Magnetic fields change direction. Only electric fields change speed.
hawking.21: Kenji operates a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT directed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Determine the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
hawking.22: Speed. Rearrange ½mv² = qV to get v = √(2qV/m). Substituting: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — below one-tenth of the speed of light, slow enough for the classical treatment.
hawking.23: Radius. Convert the field strength first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons follow a circular arc of radius 0.11 m, and the magnetic force changes their direction without changing their speed.
hawking.24: The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton combines these two ingredients on a large scale, as Figure 5.2 shows. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields raise the electrons' kinetic energy in repeated W = qV stages. A booster ring then brings them to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they are injected into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam around the circuit.
hawking.25: The booster ring holds the one piece of synchrotron physics the study design names. As the electrons' momentum mv grows, r = mv/qB demands that the radius of their path grow too — unless B grows in step. So a synchrotron raises the strength of its bending field in synchrony with the particles' momentum, holding the radius constant so the beam stays inside the one ring of magnets. This synchronisation gives the machine its name.
hawking.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons travel at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, where the classical relation r = mv/qB (valid for v ≪ c) no longer applies cleanly. Unit 4 examines why accelerator designers must build special relativity into machines like this one — and why charges forced around a circle radiate the intense synchrotron light the facility exists to produce.
hawking.27: This area of study returns to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring are both solutions of the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics differs in exactly one place — which force supplies the centripetal force.
hawking.28: Two rows matter. Neither force ever changes the speed of the object it steers, because both act perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object moves at constant speed but not constant velocity. The mass row is the one real asymmetry — the satellite's mass cancels out of its orbit, while the particle's mass remains in r = mv/qB.
hawking.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron solve the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity supplies the centripetal force for one, the magnetic force for the other, and neither force changes the object's speed.

Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil keeps its own ring forever, so the current through the coil never reverses. Slip rings belong on alternators, the AC generators you’ll meet in Area of Study 3.

So if you try to explain how a DC motor works using electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF, you’ve made a category error.

A coil set down at rest in this position would never begin to turn; a coil already spinning is carried through on its own momentum, and nothing more.

bronowski.6: The commutator is a conducting ring cleft into two halves, fastened to the axle, each half joined to one end of the coil; stationary brushes lean against the turning ring and carry the current in from the supply. Every half revolution each brush rides across the gap and passes from one half-ring to the other, and in doing so reverses the current through the coil. Reverse the current and you reverse the forces, and so the torque keeps pressing the coil round in the same direction as before.
bronowski.7: A split-ring commutator must not be confused with a pair of slip rings; they are not the same thing. Slip rings are unbroken: each end of the coil keeps its own ring for good, and the current through the coil never turns about. Fit them to a DC motor and you get exactly the failure described above — the coil rocking back and forth about the perpendicular, going nowhere. Slip rings belong instead to the alternators, the AC generators we shall meet in Area of Study 3.
bronowski.8: Key idea Every half revolution, just as the coil passes through the position where the torque vanishes, the split-ring commutator reverses the current in the coil — and by that reversal the torque is held always turning in the one same direction.
bronowski.9: Trap A motor and a generator are not one machine but two. A motor takes the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) and from it wrings rotation; a generator takes rotation and from it induces a current. To explain how a DC motor works by appealing to electromagnetic induction, to flux or to EMF, is to confuse the one machine with the other — an error of kind, not of degree.
bronowski.10: Lucia sets out to build a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil lying parallel to the field, as Figure 5.1 shows. Find the magnitude and direction of the force the magnetic field exerts on side AB, and state the magnitude of the force it exerts on side BC.
bronowski.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs perpendicular to the field, so F = nIlB is the law that governs it. Putting in the numbers, with the side length first turned into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. The force the field exerts on side AB is 0.60 N, directed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; the force it exerts on side CD is 0.60 N vertically downward.
bronowski.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs along the field, and so the magnetic force upon it is zero. Sides BC and DA add neither force nor torque; their part is only to close the circuit between the two sides that carry the force.
bronowski.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 the very same coil-in-field geometry is run backward: drive the coil round with an external torque, and it becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor turning one way turns into the component that keeps a DC generator's output voltage one way; and the slip rings come back again as the AC alternative.
bronowski.14: The study design asks no more than this: how, in a qualitative way, three quantities bear upon the torque of a simple motor. All three work through the one relation F = nIlB — whatever raises the force on the sides of the coil raises the torque with it.
bronowski.15: Take care to hold apart the force and the torque as the coil goes round. The force on each force-carrying side keeps its magnitude nIlB unchanged at every angle, for n, I, l and B care nothing for how the coil happens to be turned. It is the torque that varies: at its greatest when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, falling to nothing when it stands perpendicular.
bronowski.16: Trap Force is not torque. Through one whole revolution the force on a force-carrying side keeps its steady magnitude nIlB, turning about only at each commutation; the torque, meanwhile, rises and falls as the lever arm lengthens and shortens. Set the force against time on a graph and you have a flat line — not a sine curve.
bronowski.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is built from the same two ingredients, and only those two. A uniform electric field drives a charged particle forward in a straight line and adds to its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path, altering its direction while leaving its speed untouched.
bronowski.18: The straight-line stage is the electron gun of the electric fields topic, or that same idea written large — the linear accelerator, the linac. A charge q driven from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet sets down in its from-rest form:
bronowski.19: The bending stage is the physics of the magnetic fields topic. A particle crossing a uniform magnetic field at right angles meets a force of unchanging magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, forever square to its velocity, and so it travels in a circle of radius
bronowski.20: Because the magnetic force stands perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, it does no work upon the particle at all. Magnetic fields turn the direction; speed is the gift of electric fields alone.

Electrons are driven from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT laid perpendicular to their velocity. You may set relativistic effects aside. Find the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun, and the radius of the circular path they follow in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.

The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — below a tenth of the speed of light, slow enough still for the classical treatment to hold.

An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields lift the electrons' kinetic energy through one W = qV stage after another. A booster ring then carries them up to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they are injected into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets furnish the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam round and round the circuit.

As the electrons' momentum mv grows, r = mv/qB demands that the radius of their circular path grow with it — unless B grows in step. And so a synchrotron raises the strength of its bending field in time with the rising momentum of the particles, holding the radius fixed so that the beam keeps to the single ring of magnets. It is from this keeping-in-time, this synchrony, that the machine takes its name.

bronowski.27: This area of study brings us back to uniform circular motion. A satellite in its orbit and an electron in a storage ring are both answers to the one equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics parts company in a single place alone — in which force it is that supplies the centripetal force.

Neither force ever alters the speed of the thing it steers, for both stand perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object moves at constant speed, yet never at constant velocity. The mass row is the one genuine asymmetry — the satellite's mass cancels clean out of its orbit, while the particle's mass stays behind in r = mv/qB.

mack.5: If the current never flipped, then just past the dead spot the torque would start pushing the coil the wrong way round, and instead of rotating the coil would just rock back and forth about the perpendicular position. The split-ring commutator is the gadget that stops this from happening.
mack.6: The commutator is a conducting ring sawn into two halves, bolted to the axle, with each half wired to one end of the coil. Stationary brushes press against the spinning ring to ferry current in from the supply. Every half turn each brush steps across the gap and jumps from one half-ring to the other, flipping the current through the coil. Reversed current means reversed forces, which keeps the torque pushing the same way round — turn after turn after turn.
mack.7: Don’t confuse a split-ring commutator with a pair of slip rings — they are not the same animal. Slip rings are unbroken: each end of the coil clings to its own ring forever, so the current through the coil never reverses. Bolt slip rings onto a DC motor and you get precisely the flop described above — a coil rocking uselessly about the perpendicular position. Slip rings have their own home: alternators, the AC generators waiting in Area of Study 3.
mack.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator flips the coil current every half turn, right as the coil sails through the zero-torque position, so that the torque always pushes the same way round.
mack.9: Trap A motor and a generator are two different machines, and it’s worth keeping them straight. A motor takes the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) and turns it into rotation; a generator does the reverse, using rotation to coax a current into being. Explaining how a DC motor works by reaching for electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF isn’t a small slip — it’s the wrong machine entirely.
mack.10: Lucia is building a simple DC motor for a class demo. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as drawn in Figure 5.1. Find the magnitude and direction of the force the field exerts on side AB, and state the magnitude of the force on side BC.
mack.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs perpendicular to the field, so F = nIlB is the right tool. Plug in the numbers, with the side length turned into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. So the field pushes on side AB with 0.60 N, aimed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; side CD feels 0.60 N pointing the other way, vertically downward.
mack.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs parallel to the field, so the magnetic force on it is zero — nothing at all. Sides BC and DA chip in no force and no torque; they’re just the wiring that joins up the two sides doing the real work.
mack.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 this exact coil-in-a-field setup runs in reverse: grab the coil and twist it with an external torque, and it becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor spinning one way turns out to be the very thing that keeps a DC generator’s output voltage pointing one way — and slip rings step back onto the stage as the AC alternative.
mack.14: The study design only wants the qualitative story here — how three quantities push the torque of a simple motor up or down. And all three work through the same handle, F = nIlB: crank up the force on the coil sides and the torque climbs right along with it.
mack.15: Take a moment to separate the force from the torque as the coil turns — they behave very differently. The force on each working side holds its magnitude nIlB at every single angle, because n, I, l and B couldn’t care less how the coil is oriented. It’s the torque that does the rising and falling: biggest when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, vanishing to zero when it’s perpendicular.
mack.16: Trap Force is not torque. Over one full turn the force on a working side holds the constant magnitude nIlB, only flipping direction at each commutation; the torque, meanwhile, swells and shrinks as the lever arm changes. Plot the force magnitude against time and you get a flat line — not a sine curve.
mack.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is cooked from just two ingredients. A uniform electric field accelerates a charged particle in a straight line, pumping up its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle’s path, swinging its direction around without touching its speed.
mack.18: The straight-line stage is just the electron gun from the electric fields topic — or its grown-up cousin, the linear accelerator (linac). A charge q accelerated from rest through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet hands you in its from-rest form:
mack.19: The bending stage is the physics straight out of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field feels a force of constant magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, always pointing square across its velocity, so it loops around in a circle of radius
mack.20: Because the magnetic force sits perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, it does no work on the particle — not a joule. Magnetic fields turn you; only electric fields speed you up. That’s the whole division of labour.
mack.21: Kenji runs a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then sail into a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT pointed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Work out the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
mack.22: Speed. Rearrange ½mv² = qV and out comes v = √(2qV/m). Drop in the numbers: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — under a tenth of the speed of light, comfortably slow enough that the classical treatment still holds.
mack.23: Radius. Convert the field strength first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons swing around an arc of radius 0.11 m, with the magnetic force steering their direction and leaving their speed untouched.
mack.24: The Australian Synchrotron out in Clayton takes these same two ingredients and builds them out on a grand scale, as Figure 5.2 shows. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields jack up the electrons’ kinetic energy in repeated W = qV kicks. A booster ring then pushes them to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before firing them into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform magnetic fields that curl the beam around the loop.
mack.25: The booster ring is where the one slice of genuine synchrotron physics named in the study design lives. As the electrons’ momentum mv climbs, r = mv/qB insists the radius of their circular path climb too — unless B climbs right alongside it. So a synchrotron cranks up the strength of its bending field in lockstep with the particles’ momentum, pinning the radius in place so the beam stays tucked inside the one ring of magnets. That synchronised dance is exactly what gives the machine its name.
mack.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons are screaming around at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, where the tidy classical relation r = mv/qB (good only for v ≪ c) stops working cleanly. Unit 4 digs into why accelerator designers have no choice but to bake special relativity into machines like this one — and why charges forced around a circle blaze out the intense synchrotron light the whole facility exists to make.
mack.27: This area of study circles back to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring are, deep down, two answers to the very same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics splits in exactly one place — which force is doing the job of the centripetal force.
mack.28: Two rows really earn your attention. Neither force ever changes the speed of the thing it’s steering, because both act perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object cruises at constant speed but never constant velocity. The mass row is the one honest asymmetry — the satellite’s mass cancels clean out of its orbit, while the particle’s mass stubbornly stays put in r = mv/qB.
mack.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron crack the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity plays the part of the centripetal force for one, the magnetic force for the other, and neither force ever changes the object’s speed.
