The forces are exactly as strong as before — but here's the catch: they now pull in line with the coil, straight toward or away from the axle, so the torque drops to zero. This is the dead spot. And at that very instant the gaps in the commutator slide under the brushes and briefly cut the current. So you'd think the motor should be fine — but a coil left at rest right here would never start turning. A coil already spinning only survives the dead spot because its own momentum carries it through.
veritasium.5: Now suppose the current never flipped direction. Your instinct says the coil would just keep going — but past the dead spot the torque would start pushing it the other way round, and instead of rotating the coil would swing back and forth about the perpendicular position, like a pendulum that never escapes. The split-ring commutator is the clever fix that stops exactly this.
veritasium.6: The commutator is nothing fancier than a conducting ring split into two halves, locked to the axle, each half wired to one end of the coil. Stationary brushes press against the spinning ring and feed current in from the supply. Here's the trick: every half revolution each brush crosses the gap and jumps from one half-ring to the other, flipping the current through the coil. Reverse the current and you reverse the forces — so the torque keeps shoving the coil round the same way, turn after turn.
veritasium.7: It's tempting to lump a split-ring commutator together with a pair of slip rings, but they are not the same component. Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil keeps its own ring forever, so the current through the coil never reverses. Bolt slip rings onto a DC motor and you get precisely the failure we just described — the coil oscillating about the perpendicular position, going nowhere. Slip rings belong to alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.
veritasium.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution, right as the coil passes through the zero-torque position, so the torque always keeps acting in the same rotational direction.
veritasium.9: Trap A motor and a generator feel like the same machine run backwards, and it's easy to blur them — but they're genuinely different. A motor uses the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) to make rotation. A generator uses rotation to induce a current. So the moment you start explaining how a DC motor works in terms of electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF, you've reached for the wrong machine entirely.
veritasium.10: Lucia builds a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as shown in Figure 5.1. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force exerted on side AB by the magnetic field, and state the magnitude of the force exerted on side BC.
veritasium.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs perpendicular to the field, which is exactly the case F = nIlB is built for. Substitute, with the side length pushed into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. So the field pushes on side AB with 0.60 N, directed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; the force on side CD is the same 0.60 N pointing vertically downward.
veritasium.12: Force on BC. You might expect every side to feel a push, but the current in BC runs parallel to the field, so the magnetic force on it is exactly zero. Sides BC and DA contribute no force and no torque at all — they're just wire, completing the circuit between the two sides that do the work.
veritasium.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 this same coil-in-a-field geometry runs in reverse: stop driving it and instead twist it with an external torque, and the coil becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor turning one way turns out to be the very thing that keeps a DC generator's output voltage one way — and slip rings reappear as the AC alternative.
veritasium.14: The study design asks only for the qualitative effect three quantities have on a simple motor's torque. The trick is that all three act through the same place — F = nIlB. Anything that boosts the force on the coil sides boosts the torque.
veritasium.15: This is where people slip: don't confuse the force with the torque as the coil rotates. The force on each force-carrying side holds the same magnitude nIlB at every angle, because n, I, l and B couldn't care less which way the coil is pointing. The thing that actually changes is the torque: largest when the coil's plane lies parallel to the field, zero when it's perpendicular.
veritasium.16: Trap Force is not torque. You'd be forgiven for picturing the force rising and falling as the coil spins — but over one revolution the force on a force-carrying side keeps the constant magnitude nIlB, only flipping direction at each commutation. It's the torque that swells and shrinks as the lever arm changes. Plot the force magnitude against time and you get a flat line, not a sine curve.
veritasium.17: Every particle accelerator in this course, no matter how enormous, is built from just two ingredients. A uniform electric field accelerates a charged particle in a straight line, pumping up its kinetic energy. A uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path — changing its direction without ever touching its speed.
veritasium.18: The straight-line stage is just the electron gun from the electric fields topic, or its scaled-up cousin, the linear accelerator (linac). A charge q accelerated from rest through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet writes in its from-rest form:
veritasium.19: The bending stage is straight out of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field feels a force of constant magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, aimed always at right angles to its velocity. That perpetual sideways push is exactly what forces it onto a circle of radius
veritasium.20: Here's the part that trips people up: since the magnetic force points perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant, it does no work on the particle at all. Magnetic fields only ever change direction. If you want to change a particle's speed, you need an electric field.
veritasium.21: Kenji operates a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT directed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Determine the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
veritasium.22: Speed. All the electron's kinetic energy came from the field doing work on it, so rearrange ½mv² = qV to get v = √(2qV/m). Substituting: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — and that might sound terrifyingly fast, but it's below one-tenth of the speed of light, slow enough that the classical treatment still holds.
veritasium.23: Radius. Convert the field strength first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons curve along an arc of radius 0.11 m, and — this is the whole point — the magnetic force bends their direction without changing their speed.
veritasium.24: The Australian Synchrotron in Clayton is just these same two ingredients blown up to industrial scale, as Figure 5.2 shows. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields keep raising the electrons' kinetic energy in repeated W = qV kicks. A booster ring then drives them up to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they're injected into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform fields that bend the beam around the loop.
veritasium.25: The booster ring is where the one piece of synchrotron physics the study design names actually lives. As the electrons' momentum mv climbs, look at what r = mv/qB demands: the radius of the circular path has to grow too — unless B grows right alongside it. So a synchrotron cranks up the strength of its bending field in lockstep with the particles' momentum, pinning the radius constant so the beam stays trapped inside the single ring of magnets. That synchronisation is exactly where the machine gets its name.
veritasium.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons are screaming around at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, and here the friendly classical relation r = mv/qB (only trustworthy for v ≪ c) quietly stops working cleanly. Unit 4 digs into why accelerator designers have no choice but to build special relativity into machines like this one — and why charges whipped around a circle bleed off the intense synchrotron light the whole facility exists to make.
veritasium.27: And here's a connection that's easy to miss: this area of study circles right back to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring look like utterly different problems, but they're both solutions of the very same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics differs in exactly one place — which force gets to supply the centripetal force.
veritasium.28: Two rows in that comparison really matter. First, neither force ever changes the speed of the thing it's steering, because both push perpendicular to the velocity at every instant — so each object moves at constant speed but not constant velocity. The second is the one genuine asymmetry, and it's in the mass: the satellite's mass cancels clean out of its orbit, while the particle's mass stubbornly stays put in r = mv/qB.
veritasium.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron solve the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity supplies the centripetal force for one, the magnetic force for the other, and neither force ever changes the object's speed.

attenborough.0: Two machines bring this area of study to life. The first is the DC motor, which takes the force on a current-carrying coil and coaxes it, patiently, into ceaseless rotation. The second is the particle accelerator, which calls on electric fields to drive charged particles ever faster, and on magnetic fields to bend their flight.
attenborough.1: A DC motor performs a quiet transformation — electrical energy becomes the energy of rotation. In its simplest form it is little more than a rectangular coil of n turns, slung upon an axle between the poles of a permanent magnet and fed by a DC supply. Figure 5.1 shows it at the moment of rest, the plane of the coil lying parallel to the uniform field, waiting.
attenborough.2: Look closely at the two sides of the coil that run across the field — AB and CD in Figure 5.1. They carry equal currents, but in opposite directions, and so the field presses upon them with equal and opposite forces, each one of magnitude
attenborough.3: In the position shown, one force lifts upward, the other pushes downward, and their lines of action stand apart by the full width of the coil: together they wring the greatest possible torque from the axle. The remaining two sides, BC and DA, carry their current along the field, with it or against it, and feel no magnetic force whatsoever.
attenborough.4: A quarter turn later, the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field. The two forces are as strong as ever — but now they pull straight in line with the coil, directly toward the axle and directly away from it, and the torque falls to zero. This is the dead spot. And in that same breathless instant, the gaps in the commutator slip beneath the brushes, cutting the current for a moment. A coil set down here at rest would never stir; only a coil already spinning is carried safely through, borne along by its own momentum.
attenborough.5: Were the current never to change its direction, the torque beyond the dead spot would turn against the rotation, and the coil would fall back — rocking endlessly about the perpendicular instead of turning. The split-ring commutator forbids it.
attenborough.6: The commutator is a conducting ring, cleft into two halves and fixed to the axle, each half joined to one end of the coil; stationary brushes press against the turning ring to carry the current in from the supply. Every half revolution, each brush crosses the gap and passes from one half-ring to the other, reversing the current through the coil. Reversed current means reversed forces — and so the torque goes on acting in the very same rotational direction, faithfully, turn after turn.
attenborough.7: Take care not to mistake a split-ring commutator for a pair of slip rings. Slip rings are unbroken: each end of the coil keeps its own ring forever, and so the current through the coil never reverses. Fit them to a DC motor and you bring about the very failure just described — that hopeless rocking about the perpendicular. Slip rings belong elsewhere: to the alternators, the AC generators of Area of Study 3.
attenborough.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution, just as the coil drifts through the zero-torque position, so that the torque always acts in the same rotational direction.
attenborough.9: Trap A motor and a generator are different creatures. A motor uses the force a magnetic field exerts upon a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) to bring about rotation; a generator uses rotation to induce a current. To explain how a DC motor works by appealing to electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF is to confuse one machine for its opposite.
attenborough.10: Lucia is building a simple DC motor, to show her class. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns, each side 5.0 cm long, and it carries a current of 1.5 A through a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, the plane of the coil lying parallel to the field, as in Figure 5.1. Find the magnitude and direction of the force the field exerts on side AB, and state the magnitude of the force on side BC.
attenborough.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs across the field, so F = nIlB holds. Substituting, with the side length carried over into metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. The force the field exerts on side AB is 0.60 N, lifting vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; the force on side CD is 0.60 N, pressing vertically downward.
attenborough.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs along the field, and so the magnetic force upon it is zero. Sides BC and DA give neither force nor torque; they do nothing but complete the circuit between the sides that carry the force.
attenborough.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 this same coil-in-field geometry plays out in reverse: turned now by an outside torque, the coil becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that holds a motor turning one way becomes the very part that holds a DC generator's output voltage one way — and slip rings rise again as the AC alternative.
attenborough.14: The study design asks only this: the qualitative effect of three quantities upon the torque of a simple motor. All three work through F = nIlB — whatever strengthens the force on the coil sides strengthens the torque.
attenborough.15: Take care to keep force and torque apart as the coil turns. The force on each force-carrying side holds the same magnitude, nIlB, at every angle, for n, I, l and B care nothing for how the coil is turned. It is the torque that changes: greatest when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, and zero when it stands perpendicular.
attenborough.16: Trap Force is not torque. Across one full revolution the force on a force-carrying side keeps the constant magnitude nIlB, reversing its direction only at each commutation; the torque, meanwhile, rises and falls as the lever arm swings open and closed. Drawn against time, the force magnitude is a flat line — never a sine curve.
attenborough.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is built from just two ingredients. A uniform electric field drives a charged particle forward in a straight line, swelling its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle's path, turning it aside without ever altering its speed.
attenborough.18: The straight-line stage is the electron gun of the electric fields topic — or its grander cousin, the linear accelerator, the linac. A charge q driven from rest through a potential difference V gains kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet sets down in its from-rest form:
attenborough.19: The bending stage is the physics of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving across a uniform magnetic field feels a force of unchanging magnitude, F = qvB on formula sheet, forever at right angles to its velocity — and so it sweeps out a circle, of radius
attenborough.20: Because the magnetic force stands perpendicular to the velocity at every instant, it does no work upon the particle at all. Magnetic fields turn; only electric fields quicken. Direction is the gift of one, speed the gift of the other.
attenborough.21: Kenji is at the controls of a teaching accelerator. Electrons are driven from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then sent into a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT, set perpendicular to their velocity. You may set relativistic effects aside. Find the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun, and the radius of the circular path they follow in the field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
attenborough.22: Speed. Rearranging ½mv² = qV yields v = √(2qV/m). Substituting: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — less than a tenth of the speed of light, slow enough still for the classical treatment to hold.
attenborough.23: Radius. First, carry the field strength over: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. The electrons trace a circular arc of radius 0.11 m, the magnetic force turning their course aside without ever touching their speed.
attenborough.24: The Australian Synchrotron, at Clayton, brings these two ingredients together on a grand scale, as shown in Figure 5.2. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields lift the electrons' kinetic energy through repeated W = qV stages. A booster ring then carries them to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before they are loosed into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets furnish the uniform fields that bend the beam, gently, around the circuit.
attenborough.25: The booster ring holds the one piece of synchrotron physics the study design names. As the electrons' momentum mv grows, r = mv/qB would have the radius of their circular path grow with it — unless B rises in step. And so a synchrotron strengthens its bending field in perfect synchrony with the particles' momentum, holding the radius fast, so that the beam stays always within the one ring of magnets. From this synchrony the machine takes its name.
attenborough.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons race at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, where the classical relation r = mv/qB (true only for v ≪ c) no longer holds cleanly. Unit 4 looks at why the builders of accelerators must weave special relativity into machines such as this — and why charges driven around a circle pour out the intense synchrotron light the whole facility exists to make.
attenborough.27: This area of study returns us, at last, to uniform circular motion. A satellite in its orbit and an electron in a storage ring are both answers to one and the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics parts ways in just one place — which force it is that supplies the centripetal pull.
attenborough.28: Two rows stand out. Neither force ever alters the speed of the thing it steers, for both act perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object travels at constant speed, yet never at constant velocity. The mass row is the one true asymmetry — the satellite's mass cancels clean out of its orbit, while the particle's mass lingers on in r = mv/qB.
attenborough.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron answer the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity supplies the centripetal force for the one, the magnetic force for the other — and neither force ever changes the object's speed.

greekepic.4: After a quarter of a turn the plane of the coil stands perpendicular to the field. This is the dead spot — and in that selfsame instant the gaps within the commutator pass beneath the brushes, severing the current for a breath. greekepic.6: The commutator is a ring of conducting metal, cloven into two halves and fixed upon the axle, each half joined to one end of the coil; steadfast brushes press against the turning ring to carry the current in from the supply. A motor wields the force a magnetic field lays upon a current-bearing conductor (F = nIlB) to bring forth turning; a generator wields turning to call forth a current. cnpop.0: 注意看，这一节就靠两台机器撑场子：一个是直流电动机，它能把作用在通电线圈上的力硬生生拧成连续转动；另一个是粒子加速器，它一手用电场把带电粒子提速，一手用磁场把它们拐弯带跑。
cnpop.1: 直流电动机干的活，就是把电能变成转动的能量。最简陋的版本长这样：一个绕了 n 圈的矩形线圈，架在转轴上，塞进永磁体的两极之间，再接上直流电源。图 5.1 给你展示了标准的起手姿势——线圈所在平面跟匀强磁场平平行行地躺着。
cnpop.2: 把目光锁定线圈里跟磁场垂直跑的那两条边，也就是图 5.1 里标的 AB 和 CD。它俩载着大小相等、方向相反的电流，于是磁场给它们各来一记大小相等、方向相反的力，每个力的大小是
cnpop.3: 在图里这个位置，一个力使劲往上顶，另一个力使劲往下拽，而它俩的作用线之间隔着整整一个线圈的宽度——这一对力凑出来的，正是绕轴的最大力矩。剩下那两条边 BC 和 DA，电流要么顺着磁场要么逆着磁场跑，磁场对它们一点力都不给，妥妥的局外人。
cnpop.4: 转过四分之一圈，线圈平面就跟磁场垂直了。两个力大小一点没变，可这会儿它俩齐刷刷沿着线圈方向、笔直地朝向转轴或者背离转轴，力矩当场归零。这个位置就是传说中的死点——而且就在同一瞬间，换向器上的缺口正好滑到电刷底下，把电流咔一下掐断。线圈要是老老实实停在这儿，那它一辈子也别想自己启动;转着转着冲过来的线圈呢，全靠自己那股惯性的劲儿硬闯过去。
cnpop.5: 假如电流方向死活不变，那过了死点之后，力矩就要往反方向使劲，线圈会被甩回去，只能围着垂直位置来回晃荡，压根转不起来。而分裂环换向器，就是专治这个毛病的。
cnpop.6: 换向器说白了就是一个被劈成两半的导电环，钉死在转轴上，每一半各连着线圈的一个端头;不动的电刷死死压住这个转动的环，把电源的电流送进去。每转半圈，每个电刷就跨过那道缝、从一个半环跳到另一个半环上，线圈里的电流方向就被翻转一次。电流反向就意味着力反向，于是力矩永远朝着同一个转动方向使劲，转个不停。
cnpop.7: 千万别把分裂环换向器跟一对滑环搞混了，它俩根本不是一个零件。滑环是连续的整圈：线圈的每个端头永远霸占着自己那个环，所以线圈里的电流压根不会反向。你要是把滑环装到直流电动机上，那就会精准复刻上面说的那个翻车现场——围着垂直位置来回晃荡。滑环的归宿是交流发电机，也就是研究领域 3 里那种产生交流电的机器。
cnpop.8: Key idea 分裂环换向器在线圈每转半圈、恰好经过零力矩位置的时候，把线圈电流翻转一次，这样一来力矩就永远朝着同一个转动方向使劲。
cnpop.9: Trap 划重点别踩坑：电动机和发电机是两台完全不同的机器。电动机是用磁场作用在通电导体上的力（F = nIlB）来搞出转动;发电机是反过来，用转动去感应出电流。要是你拿电磁感应、磁通量或者电动势去解释直流电动机的工作原理，那就属于驴唇不对马嘴、概念用错了类。
cnpop.10: Lucia 为了课堂演示，亲手搭了一台简易直流电动机。她那个正方形线圈 ABCD 绕了 40 圈，边长 5.0 cm，载着 1.5 A 的电流，泡在一个 0.20 T 的匀强磁场里，线圈平面跟磁场平行，就像图 5.1 画的那样。求磁场作用在 AB 边上的力的大小和方向，并说出磁场作用在 BC 边上的力的大小。
cnpop.11: AB 边上的力。 AB 里的电流跟磁场垂直，所以 F = nIlB 可以直接上。代进去，记得先把边长换成米：F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N。磁场作用在 AB 边上的力是 0.60 N，由右手拍击定则判断，方向竖直向上;作用在 CD 边上的力是 0.60 N，竖直向下。
cnpop.12: BC 边上的力。 BC 里的电流是顺着磁场跑的，所以磁场作用在它身上的力是零。BC 和 DA 这两条边既不出力也不贡献力矩;它们只是在两条出力边之间把电路接通而已。
cnpop.13: Connects 到了研究领域 3，同一套“线圈泡在磁场里”的几何结构会倒着跑：外力拧着线圈转，这线圈摇身一变就成了发电机。那个让电动机一个方向转下去的分裂环换向器，到这儿就变成了让直流发电机输出电压一个方向输出的零件;而滑环，则作为交流那条路线的备选方案再次登场。
cnpop.14: 课程标准只要求你定性说出三个量对简易电动机力矩的影响。这三个量全都是通过 F = nIlB 起作用的：凡是能加大线圈各边受力的，都能加大力矩。
cnpop.15: 线圈转动的时候，一定要把力和力矩仔仔细细分清楚。每条出力边上的力，在任何角度都死死保持着 nIlB 这个大小，因为 n、I、l 和 B 压根不看线圈的朝向。真正会变的是力矩：线圈平面跟磁场平行时最大，垂直时归零。
cnpop.16: Trap 力不是力矩，别画等号。转一整圈下来，出力边上的力一直保持着 nIlB 这个恒定大小，只在每次换向那一刻翻一下方向;而力矩则随着力臂的变化忽上忽下。要是把力的大小对时间画出来，那是一条平平的直线，可不是什么正弦曲线。
cnpop.17: 本课程里的每一台粒子加速器，说到底都是两味原料搭出来的。一个匀强电场把带电粒子沿直线提速，给它灌进动能;一个匀强磁场把粒子的路径掰弯，只改方向、不改快慢。
cnpop.18: 那个直线提速的环节，就是电场那一节里讲过的电子枪，或者它的放大加强版——直线加速器（linac）。一个电荷 q 从静止出发、被电势差 V 一路加速，会获得动能 W = qV not on sheet，而公式表把它写成了从静止出发的那个形式：
cnpop.19: 那个掰弯路径的环节，正是磁场那一节的物理。一个垂直于匀强磁场运动的粒子，会受到一个大小恒定的力 F = qvB on formula sheet，这个力永远跟速度成直角，于是粒子就沿着一个圆跑，半径为
cnpop.20: 正因为磁力在每一瞬间都跟速度垂直，它对粒子根本不做功。磁场负责改方向;只有电场才能改快慢。记牢了。
cnpop.21: Kenji 在操作一台教学用加速器。电子从静止出发，被一个 2.5 kV 的电势差加速，然后冲进一个磁感应强度为 1.5 mT、方向垂直于它们速度的匀强磁场区。相对论效应你可以先忽略不管。求电子离开电子枪时的速度，以及它们在磁场中做圆周运动的半径，电子质量 m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg、电荷量 q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C 取自公式表。
cnpop.22: 速度。 把 ½mv² = qV 整理一下，得到 v = √(2qV/m)。代进去：v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹。电子离开电子枪时的速度是 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹——还不到光速的十分之一，慢到完全可以用经典那套来处理。
cnpop.23: 半径。 先把磁感应强度换算一下：1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T。然后 r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m。电子沿着一段半径为 0.11 m 的圆弧跑，磁力只改它们的方向、不改它们的快慢。
cnpop.24: 位于 Clayton 的澳大利亚同步加速器，就是把这两味原料放大到巨型规模玩，参见图 5.2。一把电子枪给直线加速器（linac）喂料，电场在那里通过一轮又一轮的 W = qV 把电子的动能往上抬。接着一个增强环把它们提到 3.0 GeV 的最终能量，然后再注入那个周长 216 m 的储存环，里面的二极磁体提供匀强磁场，把束流沿着整圈轨道弯着带过来。
cnpop.25: 增强环里藏着课程标准点名要讲的那一块同步加速器物理。随着电子的动量 mv 越长越大，r = mv/qB 就要求它们圆周路径的半径也跟着变大——除非 B 也同步跟上来一起涨。所以同步加速器的玩法，就是让弯转磁场的强度跟粒子动量同步增大，把半径死死摁住不变，好让束流乖乖待在那一圈磁体里头。机器的名字“同步”就是这么来的。
cnpop.26: Connects 在 3.0 GeV 这个能量上，储存环里的电子跑得超过了光速的 99.999%，这时候那条经典关系式 r = mv/qB（只在 v ≪ c 时管用）就不能干干净净地照搬了。单元 4 会研究为什么加速器的设计师必须把狭义相对论塞进这种机器里——以及为什么被逼着绕圈跑的电荷，会辐射出那种强烈的同步辐射光，而整座设施存在的意义就是为了产出这种光。
cnpop.27: 这个研究领域又绕回了匀速圆周运动。一颗绕轨道转的卫星，和一个在储存环里跑的电子，本质上都是同一个方程的解：Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet。两者的物理就差在一个地方——到底是哪个力来当向心力。
cnpop.28: 有两行特别值得拎出来说。无论哪个力，都从来不会改变它所操控对象的快慢，因为它俩在每一瞬间都跟速度垂直：每个对象都以恒定的快慢运动，但速度（矢量）可不是恒定的。质量那一行才是唯一真正的不对称之处——卫星的质量在它的轨道里被约掉、消失得无影无踪，而粒子的质量却稳稳留在 r = mv/qB 里。
cnpop.29: Key idea 一颗卫星和一个储存环里的电子，解的是同一个方程：Fnet = mv²/r。重力给前者当向心力，磁力给后者当向心力，而这两个力谁都不会改变对象的快慢。

ai.0: Two machines bring this whole area of study to life. There’s the DC motor, which takes the force on a current-carrying coil and spins it into endless rotation — and the particle accelerator, which leans on electric fields to whip charged particles faster and magnetic fields to steer them where you want them to go.
ai.1: A DC motor takes electrical energy and turns it — literally — into the energy of rotation. Strip it back to the simplest version and you’re left with a rectangular coil of n turns, mounted on an axle between the poles of a permanent magnet and wired to a DC supply. Figure 5.1 catches it in the standard starting position, with the plane of the coil lying parallel to the uniform field.
ai.2: Look at the two sides of the coil that run perpendicular to the field — AB and CD in Figure 5.1. They carry equal currents flowing in opposite directions, so the field pushes on them with equal and opposite forces, each one of magnitude
ai.3: In the position drawn, one force shoves vertically up and the other vertically down, and their lines of action sit a full coil-width apart: that pairing delivers the maximum torque about the axle. The other two sides, BC and DA, carry their current along the field (or against it), so they feel no magnetic force whatsoever.
ai.4: Give it a quarter turn and the plane of the coil swings perpendicular to the field. The two forces haven’t changed in size at all, but now they pull straight in line with the coil — directly toward or away from the axle — so the torque drops to zero. This is the dead spot. And right at that same instant, the gaps in the commutator slide under the brushes and briefly cut the current. Park a coil here and it would never get going; a coil already spinning, though, just coasts on through on its own momentum.
ai.5: If the current never flipped direction, the torque just past the dead spot would start pushing the coil the wrong way round, and instead of rotating it would simply swing back, rocking forever about the perpendicular position. The split-ring commutator is what stops that from happening.
ai.6: The commutator is a conducting ring sliced into two halves and fixed to the axle, with each half wired to one end of the coil; stationary brushes press against the spinning ring to feed current in from the supply. Every half revolution, each brush hops across the gap and switches from one half-ring to the other — reversing the current through the coil. Reversed current means reversed forces, which keeps the torque pushing in the same rotational direction.
ai.7: Don’t confuse a split-ring commutator with a pair of slip rings — they’re not the same component at all. Slip rings are continuous: each end of the coil hangs on to its own ring permanently, so the current through the coil never reverses. Bolt slip rings onto a DC motor and you get exactly the failure described above — oscillation about the perpendicular position. Slip rings belong elsewhere: in alternators, the AC generators you’ll meet in Area of Study 3.
ai.8: Key idea The split-ring commutator reverses the coil current every half revolution, just as the coil sweeps through the zero-torque position, so the torque always keeps acting in the same rotational direction.
ai.9: Trap A motor and a generator are different machines — don’t blur them together. A motor uses the force a magnetic field exerts on a current-carrying conductor (F = nIlB) to produce rotation; a generator does the reverse, using rotation to induce a current. So explaining how a DC motor works by reaching for electromagnetic induction, flux or EMF is a genuine category error.
ai.10: Lucia is building a simple DC motor for a class demonstration. Her square coil ABCD has 40 turns of side length 5.0 cm and carries a current of 1.5 A in a uniform magnetic field of 0.20 T, with the plane of the coil parallel to the field, as shown in Figure 5.1. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the force the magnetic field exerts on side AB, and state the magnitude of the force it exerts on side BC.
ai.11: Force on AB. The current in AB runs perpendicular to the field, so F = nIlB is the formula you want. Substitute, converting the side length to metres: F = 40 × 1.5 A × 0.050 m × 0.20 T = 0.60 N. So the field exerts a force of 0.60 N on side AB, directed vertically upward by the right-hand slap rule; the force on side CD comes out at 0.60 N vertically downward.
ai.12: Force on BC. The current in BC runs parallel to the field, so the magnetic force on it is zero. Sides BC and DA chip in no force and no torque at all; they’re simply there to close the circuit between the two force-carrying sides.
ai.13: Connects In Area of Study 3 this very same coil-in-field geometry runs backwards: drive the coil with an external torque and it becomes a generator. The split-ring commutator that keeps a motor spinning one way turns into the component that keeps a DC generator’s output voltage pointing one way — and slip rings reappear as the AC alternative.
ai.14: The study design only asks you for the qualitative effect of three quantities on the torque of a simple motor. All three work through the same equation, F = nIlB: anything that boosts the force on the coil sides boosts the torque.
ai.15: Keep a sharp line between the force and the torque as the coil turns. The force on each force-carrying side holds the same magnitude nIlB at every angle, because n, I, l and B don’t care which way the coil is pointing. It’s the torque that does the changing: maximum when the plane of the coil lies parallel to the field, zero when it sits perpendicular.
ai.16: Trap Force is not torque. Over one revolution the force on a force-carrying side holds its constant magnitude nIlB, flipping direction only at each commutation; meanwhile the torque rises and falls as the lever arm shifts. Plot the force magnitude against time and you get a flat line — not a sine curve.
ai.17: Every particle accelerator in this course is put together from just two ingredients. A uniform electric field accelerates a charged particle in a straight line, pumping up its kinetic energy; a uniform magnetic field bends the particle’s path, swinging its direction around without touching its speed.
ai.18: The straight-line stage is the electron gun you met in the electric fields topic — or its scaled-up cousin, the linear accelerator (linac). A charge q accelerated from rest through a potential difference V picks up kinetic energy W = qV not on sheet, which the formula sheet writes in its from-rest form:
ai.19: The bending stage is the physics of the magnetic fields topic. A particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field feels a force of constant magnitude F = qvB on formula sheet, always pointing at right angles to its velocity, so it sweeps out a circle of radius
ai.20: Because the magnetic force stays perpendicular to the velocity at every single instant, it does no work on the particle. Magnetic fields change direction; only electric fields change speed.
ai.21: Kenji runs a teaching accelerator. Electrons are accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 2.5 kV, then enter a region of uniform magnetic field of strength 1.5 mT directed perpendicular to their velocity. You may ignore relativistic effects. Determine the speed of the electrons as they leave the gun and the radius of their circular path in the magnetic field, taking m<sub>e</sub> = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg and q<sub>e</sub> = 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C from the formula sheet.
ai.22: Speed. Rearrange ½mv² = qV and you get v = √(2qV/m). Substituting: v = √((2 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 2.5 × 10³ V) / (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg)) = √(8.78 × 10¹⁴ m² s⁻²) = 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹. The electrons leave the gun at 3.0 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹ — under one-tenth of the speed of light, slow enough to trust the classical treatment.
ai.23: Radius. Convert the field strength first: 1.5 mT = 1.5 × 10⁻³ T. Then r = mv/qB = (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg × 2.96 × 10⁷ m s⁻¹) / (1.60 × 10⁻¹⁹ C × 1.5 × 10⁻³ T) = 0.11 m. So the electrons curve along a circular arc of radius 0.11 m, with the magnetic force bending their direction and leaving their speed untouched.
ai.24: The Australian Synchrotron out in Clayton brings these two ingredients together on a grand scale, as Figure 5.2 shows. An electron gun feeds a linac, where electric fields haul the electrons’ kinetic energy up in repeated W = qV stages. A booster ring then drives them to a final energy of 3.0 GeV before injecting them into the 216 m storage ring, where dipole magnets supply the uniform magnetic fields that bend the beam around the loop.
ai.25: The booster ring is home to the one piece of synchrotron physics the study design actually names. As the electrons’ momentum mv climbs, r = mv/qB demands that the radius of their circular path climb too — unless B climbs right alongside it. So a synchrotron ramps up the strength of its bending field in step with the particles’ momentum, pinning the radius constant so the beam stays tucked inside the one ring of magnets. That synchronisation is exactly where the machine gets its name.
ai.26: Connects At 3.0 GeV the storage-ring electrons are travelling at more than 99.999% of the speed of light, where the classical relation r = mv/qB (good only for v ≪ c) no longer holds cleanly. Unit 4 digs into why accelerator designers have to build special relativity right into machines like this one — and why charges forced around a circle pour out the intense synchrotron light the whole facility exists to make.
ai.27: This area of study circles back to uniform circular motion. A satellite in orbit and an electron in a storage ring turn out to be two solutions of the very same equation, Fnet = mv²/r on formula sheet. The physics differs in exactly one spot — which force is doing the job of the centripetal force.
ai.28: Two rows really matter here. Neither force ever changes the speed of the object it’s steering, because both act perpendicular to the velocity at every instant: each object cruises at constant speed but never constant velocity. The mass row is the one genuine asymmetry — the satellite’s mass cancels clean out of its orbit, while the particle’s mass stubbornly stays put in r = mv/qB.
ai.29: Key idea A satellite and a storage-ring electron solve the same equation, Fnet = mv²/r. Gravity supplies the centripetal force for one, the magnetic force for the other — and neither force ever changes the object’s speed.
